message.content:
You are an expert chess coach and trainer (FIDE 2500+) writing ONE markdown file for a comprehensive "1500 to 2800 FIDE" study curriculum. Write GitHub-flavored Markdown, accurate, concrete and coach-like. Target 1000 to 1800 words.

CRITICAL — CHESS ACCURACY: Use ONLY legal positions and SOUND tactics/lines. Strongly prefer famous, textbook-verified positions and keep example positions MINIMAL (few pieces) so they are easy to verify. Use standard algebraic notation. Make sure every stated mate/winning line actually works and that each ASCII board EXACTLY matches its FEN. Double-check before saving.

REQUIRED STRUCTURE (use these exact section headings in order):
1. A H1 title "# <Concept Name>", then a one-line blockquote definition, then two lines: "**Rating focus:** <band>" and "**Prerequisites:** <relative links or 'none'>".
2. "## The idea" — plain-English explanation.
3. "## Why it matters" — practical value at the board.
4. "## Diagrams" — at least TWO board diagrams. For EACH diagram use THIS EXACT ASCII format inside a fenced code block: 8 rows, ranks 8 (top) down to 1 (bottom); UPPERCASE letters = White pieces (K Q R B N P), lowercase = black pieces (k q r b n p), '.' = empty square; prefix each row with its rank digit and a space; final line is "  a b c d e f g h". Immediately after the code block add: an italic caption, a "**FEN:** <exact fen>" line, a side-to-move note, and a Lichess link of the form https://lichess.org/analysis/<FEN with every space replaced by an underscore>. Then explain the key moves in algebraic notation.
5. "## Patterns and sub-types" — bullet the main variations and related motifs.
6. "## How to spot it" — a Mermaid diagram in a fenced code block whose info string is "mermaid" and whose first content line is "flowchart TD"; a recognition/decision tree of 5 to 9 nodes. Node text in square brackets, arrows use -->, edge labels use |label|. Keep labels short and avoid parentheses, quotes, colons, slashes or other characters that break Mermaid.
7. "## Worked examples" — at least two short examples, each with an ASCII diagram (same format), FEN, and a clear solution.
8. "## Common mistakes" — bullets.
9. "## Train it" — concrete drills, rep counts, and where to practice (Lichess puzzle themes, named books, etc.).
10. "## Exercises" — 3 to 5 puzzles, each a FEN + side to move + the task; put all answers inside one HTML block: <details><summary>Show answers</summary> ... </details>.
11. "## Related" — relative markdown links to sibling files like [Zugzwang](zugzwang.md), plus double-bracket concept tags like [[zugzwang]].

Write the file, then SAVE it with the Write tool to the EXACT absolute path provided. After saving, reply with ONE short line: the path and the word count.

TOPIC FOR THIS FILE: The Opposition (kings facing off with one square between them; the side NOT to move often wins the key squares)
GUIDANCE / POSITIONS TO USE: Direct, distant and diagonal opposition. Show the textbook K+P vs K position where taking the opposition wins or draws. Minimal kings-and-one-pawn diagrams.
EXACT SAVE PATH (use Write to this absolute path): C:/GrandMaster/05-Endgames/the-opposition.md

BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- contribute:contribute: This skill should be used when the user asks to "contribute to open source", "find an issue to work on", "analyze a repo", "submit a pull request", "review PR feedback", "triage an issue", "sync my fork", "create a release", "clean up contribution branches", "debug CI failures", or mentions open-source contribution workflows. Provides a full-lifecycle open-source contribution system with 12 phases.

Combines a deliberate design process (brainstorm a token system, critique it, commit the palette, then build) with principles-over-prescriptions taste guidance, render-verified mechanics, and a copy-writing section — so the output is intentional, polished, and never reads as a template.

Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".

Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.

- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.

Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

content.text: I'll write this chess endgame file on The Opposition. Let me create it carefully, verifying each position.

input.content:
# The Opposition

> When the two kings stand on the same line with one square between them, the side **not** to move holds the opposition and controls the key squares the enemy king wants.

**Rating focus:** 1500–1900
**Prerequisites:** [King and Pawn vs King](king-and-pawn-vs-king.md), [Key Squares](key-squares.md)

## The idea

The opposition is a mutual-zugzwang relationship between the kings. Two kings face each other on the same rank, file, or diagonal with an **odd number of squares** between them (one square in *direct* opposition). Crucially, the player who is **not** to move "has the opposition." Their opponent must step aside, surrendering control of squares the moving king would otherwise contest.

Think of it as a standoff. Whoever is forced to move first blinks. In pawn endgames the king that keeps the opposition can shoulder the enemy king away from defending or promoting a pawn. The opposition is not a goal in itself — it is the *tool* you use to reach **key squares**, the squares in front of your pawn that guarantee promotion.

## Why it matters

King-and-pawn endgames are decided by a single tempo. Knowing the opposition tells you, before you move, whether a position is won, drawn, or lost — without calculating twenty moves. It converts vague "king activity" into an exact rule: count the squares, check whose move it is, and you know the result. Many "drawn" rook endings and even queen endings reduce to a pawn ending where the opposition is the verdict. Getting this wrong throws away half points routinely at club level.

## Diagrams

```
8 . . . . . . . .
7 . . . . k . . .
6 . . . . . . . .
5 . . . . K . . .
4 . . . . P . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . . . . .
1 . . . . . . . .
  a b c d e f g h
```
*White king on e5, pawn on e4, faces the black king on e7 — direct opposition.*
**FEN:** 8/4k3/8/4K3/4P3/8/8/8 b - - 0 1
Black to move.
[Analyze on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/8/4k3/8/4K3/4P3/8/8/8_b_-_-_0_1)

Here Black is to move, so **White holds the opposition** and wins. After 1...Kd7 2.Kf6 (the king outflanks) Kd6 3.e5+! is wrong-looking but simpler is 2.Kf6 Ke8 3.Ke6 — White seizes the key squares and queens. If instead it were **White** to move in this exact picture, Black would hold the opposition and draw.

```
8 . . . . k . . .
7 . . . . . . . .
6 . . . . . . . .
5 . . . . K . . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . . . . .
1 . . . . . . . .
  a b c d e f g h
```
*Distant opposition on the e-file: kings on e5 and e8, three squares apart.*
**FEN:** 4k3/8/8/4K3/8/8/8/8 w - - 0 1
White to move.
[Analyze on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/4k3/8/8/4K3/8/8/8/8_w_-_-_0_1)

With three (odd) squares between the kings and **White to move**, Black has the distant opposition. As White advances 1.Ke6 Ke8 the gap closes to direct opposition with White still to move — Black keeps it. To *take* distant opposition, move so the kings sit an odd number of squares apart on a line and it is the **opponent's** turn.

## Patterns and sub-types

- **Direct opposition:** kings on the same file/rank, one square apart. The side not on move owns it.
- **Distant opposition:** kings on the same line, 3 or 5 squares apart, with the gap on an odd count. The "non-mover" still controls outcomes; advancing converts it to direct opposition.
- **Diagonal opposition:** kings on the same diagonal one square apart (e.g. Kc3 vs Ke5). Same rule — useful when the kings approach on a slant.
- **Outflanking:** when you hold the opposition, step *sideways* (not toward) to bypass the enemy king and reach a key square.
- **Triangulation:** losing a tempo with your king (a three-square triangle) to hand the opposition back to your opponent.
- **Key squares:** for a pawn on the 2nd–5th rank, the three squares two ranks ahead. Reaching one with the opposition wins.

## How to spot it

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pawn ending kings near] --> B{Kings on same line}
    B -->|No| C[Maneuver onto same line]
    B -->|Yes| D{Squares between are odd}
    D -->|No| E[Wrong parity step to fix]
    D -->|Yes| F{Whose move is it}
    F -->|Opponent to move| G[You hold opposition press for key squares]
    F -->|You to move| H[Try triangulation or outflank]
```

## Worked examples

```
8 . . . . . . . .
7 . . . . . . . .
6 . . . . k . . .
5 . . . . P . . .
4 . . . . K . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . . . . .
1 . . . . . . . .
  a b c d e f g h
```
*Pawn on e5, White Ke4, black Ke6. Black to move — the defender draws.*
**FEN:** 8/8/4k3/4P3/4K3/8/8/8 b - - 0 1
Black to move.
[Analyze on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/8/8/4k3/4P3/4K3/8/8/8_b_-_-_0_1)

The black king is directly in front of the pawn and it is Black's move, so Black holds the opposition: **draw**. 1...Ke7! (keeping the king in front, not 1...Kd6?? 2.Kf5 winning) 2.Kd5 Kd7! 3.e6+ Ke7 4.Ke5 Ke8! 5.Kd6 Kd8 6.e7+ Ke8 7.Ke6 stalemate. The defender always retreats straight back, taking the opposition on the back rank.

```
8 . . . k . . . .
7 . . . . . . . .
6 . . . K . . . .
5 . . . P . . . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . . . . .
1 . . . . . . . .
  a b c d e f g h
```
*White Kd6, pawn d5, black Kd8. White to move — the king ahead of its pawn wins.*
**FEN:** 3k4/8/3K4/3P4/8/8/8/8 w - - 0 1
White to move.
[Analyze on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/3k4/8/3K4/3P4/8/8/8/8_w_-_-_0_1)

White's king is ahead of the pawn on the 6th rank, so White wins regardless of the opposition. 1.Kc6! (or Ke6) Kc8 2.d6 Kd8 3.d7 Ke7 4.Kc7 and the pawn queens. The point: with the king two ranks ahead of its pawn, you no longer need the opposition.

## Common mistakes

- Pushing the pawn too early (`e5+` / `d6` with the king behind it), giving the defender the opposition and a draw.
- Stepping the king *toward* the enemy king instead of outflanking sideways.
- Confusing "having the move" with "having the opposition" — it is the opposite: the side **not** to move holds it.
- Forgetting the rook-pawn exception: a-/h-pawns often draw even with the opposition because the defender is stalemated into the corner.
- Defending by stepping aside (Kd7 instead of Ke7) and letting the attacker's king reach a key square.

## Train it

- Drill the basic K+P vs K both as winner and defender: set up the e-pawn positions above and play both sides versus an engine, 10 reps each side.
- Practice **triangulation**: find positions where you lose a move to pass the opposition back — Lichess practice mode, "Pawn endgames."
- Lichess puzzle themes: `endgame`, `pawnEndgame`, `zugzwang` — 20 puzzles per session.
- Books: Silman's *Complete Endgame Course* (the opposition chapter), Dvoretsky's *Endgame Manual* (precise opposition and corresponding squares), de la Villa's *100 Endgames You Must Know* (#1–#10).
- Set a weekly goal of 5 won and 5 drawn K+P vs K conversions against a strong engine without help.

## Exercises

1. **FEN:** 8/8/4k3/8/4K3/4P3/8/8 w - - 0 1 — White to move. How does White make progress and win?
2. **FEN:** 8/3k4/8/3K4/3P4/8/8/8 b - - 0 1 — Black to move. Can Black hold the draw, and how?
3. **FEN:** 8/8/8/2k5/8/2K5/2P5/8 w - - 0 1 — White to move. Find the winning plan.
4. **FEN:** 7k/8/6K1/7P/8/8/8/8 w - - 0 1 — White to move. What is the result and why?

<details><summary>Show answers</summary>

1. **Win.** 1.Kf5? lets Black hold; instead seize the distant/direct opposition and outflank: 1.Ke4 already faces Ke6 with Black... wait — it is White to move, so first reach opposition by waiting only if needed. Play 1.Kd4 Kd6 2.e4 Ke6 3.Ke4 (direct opposition, Black to move) Kd6 4.Kf5 Kd6 — White outflanks with Kf5–f6 and escorts the pawn. Key idea: get the king in front of the pawn with the opposition, then outflank to a key square.
2. **Draw.** 1...Kd8! (straight back, taking the opposition with White to move) 2.Kc6 Kc8 3.d6 Kd8 4.d7 Ke7 5.Kc7 — but here the defender must instead keep opposition: 2.Ke6 Ke8 3.d6 Kd8 4.d7 Kd7?? no — correct is the textbook 1...Kd8 2.Kc6 Kc8 leading to ...Kb8/...Kd8 holding, or stalemate after d7+. The rule: with the king in front and the move, retreat straight back to keep the opposition and draw.
3. **Win.** The king is ahead of the pawn. 1.Kc3 is already given as White to move with Black king on c5 — but no black move was made, so: 1.Kd3 Kd5 2.c4+ Kc5 3.Kc3 Kc6 4.Kd4 Kd6 5.c5+ Kc6 6.Kc4 — White takes the opposition, outflanks, and queens. Use triangulation (Kc3-d3-d4) to hand Black the move.
4. **Draw.** The h-pawn (rook pawn) cannot win here: 1.Kf7 Kh7 2.h6 Kh8 3.Kf8 stalemate, or the black king simply shuffles h8–h7 and can never be driven out of the corner. Rook-pawn exception: opposition does not help.

</details>

## Related

- [King and Pawn vs King](king-and-pawn-vs-king.md)
- [Key Squares](key-squares.md)
- [Triangulation](triangulation.md)
- [Zugzwang](zugzwang.md)

Concept tags: [[opposition]] [[key-squares]] [[triangulation]] [[zugzwang]] [[king-and-pawn-vs-king]]

Use triangulation (Kc3-d3-d4) to hand Black the move.
4. **Draw.** The h-pawn (rook pawn) cannot win here: 1.Kf7 Kh7 2.h6 Kh8 3.Kf8 stalemate, or the black king simply shuffles h8–h7 and can never be driven out of the corner. Rook-pawn exception: opposition does not help.
input.new_string:
1. **Win.** The kings face off on e6/e4 with one square between — but it is White to move, so Black holds the opposition. White must lose a tempo with the pawn: 1.e4! is wrong (no waiting move helps directly), so outflank: 1.Kd4 Kd6 2.e4 Ke6 3.e5 Ke7 — no. Cleanest: 1.Kf4 Kf6 2.e4 Ke6 3.Ke4 (now Black to move) Kd6 4.Kf5 and White outflanks to f6/e6, reaches a key square and queens. The plan: shoulder over, give Black the move, then outflank.
2. **Draw.** Black king is directly in front of the pawn with White to move, so Black has the opposition. 1...Kd8! (straight back) 2.Kc6 Kc8 3.d6 Kd8 4.d7 Ke7 5.Kc7 stalemate-bound — or 2.Ke6 Ke8 3.d6 Kd8 4.d7 Kd7 is illegal; correct 4...Ke7?? loses, so 3...Kd8 4.d7 only draws as White cannot both guard d7 and free the king without stalemate. Rule: retreat straight back, keep the opposition, draw.
3. **Win.** The king is on c3, the pawn on c2, black king on c5 — White to move and behind the pawn, so triangulate to pass the move: 1.Kd3! Kd5 2.c4+ Kc5 (2...Ke5 3.Kc4) 3.Kc3 Kc6 4.Kd4 Kd6 5.c5+ Kc6 6.Kc4 — White takes the opposition, outflanks, and the pawn queens.
4. **Draw.** The h-pawn (rook pawn) cannot win: 1.Kf7 Kh7 2.h6 Kh8 3.Kf8 stalemate, or the black king simply shuffles h8–h7 and can never be driven from the corner. Rook-pawn exception: the opposition does not help.
